summary:
The Exxon Valdez oil spill occurred on March 24, 1989, when the supertanker Exxon Valdez ran aground on Bligh Reef in Prince William Sound, Alaska, spilling approximately 11 million gallons of crude oil. The spill contaminated over 1,300 miles of Alaskan coastline and caused catastrophic damage to the region's ecosystems, killing an estimated 250,000 seabirds, 2,800 sea otters, 300 harbor seals, and destroying vast quantities of fish and marine life. The captain, Joseph Hazelwood, was found to have been drinking before the accident.

The spill exposed the inadequacy of oil industry emergency response preparations and the regulatory frameworks governing tanker operations. Exxon's cleanup response was widely criticized as too slow and insufficient. The company ultimately spent over $2 billion on cleanup operations and paid $900 million in damages — though litigation over punitive damages continued for nearly two decades, with the Supreme Court ultimately capping the punitive award.

The Exxon Valdez disaster significantly accelerated environmental regulation of the oil industry. The Oil Pollution Act of 1990 mandated double-hull tanker construction, improved response planning requirements, and increased liability limits. The spill galvanized public environmental consciousness in the United States and contributed to the growth of environmental activism. It remains one of the most studied environmental disasters in history and a reference point in discussions of corporate environmental liability.
